did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) based on VGG-16 and VGG-19 architectures, employing supervised, supervised transfer learning, and semi-supervised transfer learning methods to classify skin cancer stages (solar lentigo, lentigo maligna, and lentigo maligna melanoma) from dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study conducted at the 2022 Biomedical Engineering International Conference (BMEiCON-2022). It details a specific methodology using supervised, supervised transfer learning, and semi-supervised transfer learning with VGG-16 and VGG-19 CNNs to classify skin cancer stages. The paper reports new quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score) from ten experiments on a dataset of 1742 dermoscopic images, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Using Semi-supervised Transfer Learning for Classification of Solar Lentigo, Lentigo Maligna, and Lentigo Maligna Melanoma
brief_summary: This study aimed to classify three stages of skin cancer—solar lentigo, lentigo maligna, and lentigo maligna melanoma—using convolutional neural networks based on VGG-16 and VGG-19 architectures. The researchers utilized a dataset of 1742 dermoscopic images sourced from open databases like ISIC and HAM10000, comprising both labeled and unlabeled data. The evaluation compared supervised learning, supervised transfer learning, and semi-supervised transfer learning methods, with and without data augmentation. Results indicated that semi-supervised transfer learning significantly outperformed other methods, achieving an accuracy of 0.98 with VGG-19. The study concluded that semi-supervised transfer learning based on VGG-19 provides the best prediction performance for these specific skin cancer stages.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three stages of skin cancer classified in the study. 260 labeled images and 121 unlabeled images (from ISIC) were used.
One of the three stages of skin cancer classified in the study. 107 labeled images and 121 unlabeled images (from ISIC) were used.
One of the three stages of skin cancer classified in the study. 62 labeled images and 1192 unlabeled images (from HAM10000 and other sources) were used.

Mentioned as a precursor lesion or category for unlabeled data in the dataset (121 images from ISIC labeled as 'in situ' in the unlabeled set), but not a primary target class for the classification task.

Mentioned as a category for unlabeled data (1192 images from HAM10000 and other sources labeled as 'melanoma') and in the context of general skin cancer mortality, but not a primary target class for the specific three-class classification task.

all_task_types.target: Three discrete stages of skin cancer: solar lentigo (SL), lentigo maligna (LM), and lentigo maligna melanoma (LMM).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the intention to 'classify three stages of skin cancer' using CNNs (VGG-16 and VGG-19). The task is a multiclass classification problem with three distinct diagnostic categories. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermoscopic Dataset (ISIC, HAM10000, and Other Sources)
datasets_used.description: A combined dataset of 1742 dermoscopic images obtained from open-source databases including the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive, the HAM10000 dataset, and other websites/resources. The dataset was split into labeled images for supervised training and unlabeled images for semi-supervised training.

use.training: The labeled dataset was randomly split into 85% (365 images) for training. The unlabeled dataset (1313 images) was used for semi-supervised training to generate pseudo-labels.
use.testing: The labeled dataset was randomly split into 15% (64 images) for testing.
use.other: Unlabeled data from ISIC (121 images of 'in situ') and HAM10000/other sources (1192 images of 'melanoma') were used for semi-supervised learning.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess model performance and compute sensitivity, specificity, F1-score, and accuracy for each experimental condition.
To compute the overall sensitivity and specificity metrics across the three classes (Solar Lentigo, Lentigo Maligna, Lentigo Maligna Melanoma).
To select unlabeled data for semi-supervised training by filtering predictions with probabilities greater than or equal to 80%.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score) derived from confusion matrices on a fixed test set.

Custom Dermoscopic Dataset (ISIC, HAM100.notes: Semi-supervised transfer learning based on VGG-19 achieved the best prediction performance. Data augmentation generally decreased classification capability across all methods. The SSTLM with VGG-19 model predicted all classes correctly except for one LMM image which was misclassified as SL.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models used for transfer learning (specific weights not detailed, but standard ImageNet pretraining is implied by 'transfer learning' context in related work section)

architecture_details.layer_modification: Last layer removed for transfer learning; rest of weights used as fixed feature extractor
architecture_details.implementation: Keras and TensorFlow on Google Colab
architecture_details.model_selection: Autokeras used to select the specific CNN model

training_details.augmentation: Random rotation up to 90 degrees and random horizontal flipping (applied in SLM + Aug, STLM + Aug, SSTLM + Aug)

classification_or_output_head.type: Custom classification head added after removing original last layer

preprocessing_techniques: Data balancing via upsampling and downsampling
Data augmentation: random rotation up to 90 degrees

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated up to 90 degrees.

conclusions: Semi-supervised transfer learning (SSTLM) based on the VGG-19 architecture achieved the best prediction performance for classifying solar lentigo, lentigo maligna, and lentigo maligna melanoma, with an accuracy of 0.98, sensitivity of 0.98, specificity of 1.00, and F1-score of 0.98.

Semi-supervised transfer learning significantly outperformed supervised learning (accuracy 0.47) and supervised transfer learning (accuracy 0.72), demonstrating the value of utilizing unlabeled medical data to improve classification accuracy when labeled data is limited.

Data augmentation (random rotation and horizontal flipping) negatively impacted the classification capability of all methods, lowering accuracy across supervised learning, supervised transfer learning, and semi-supervised transfer learning models.
The study acknowledges a limitation that the models are specific to predicting these three skin cancer stages and may not generalize to other cases or datasets.

Future work should focus on practical application by integrating hospital databases to enhance training sets and determine the model's ability to assist doctors in actual diagnosis and treatment decisions.
important_remarks: The study is limited to classifying only three specific skin cancer stages (Solar Lentigo, Lentigo Maligna, and Lentigo Maligna Melanoma) and may not generalize to other cases or datasets.

Data augmentation (random rotation and horizontal flipping) negatively impacted classification performance, lowering accuracy across all methods (SLM, STLM, and SSTLM), suggesting potential underfitting or loss of critical features for this specific task.
The dataset is heavily imbalanced, with 1192 unlabeled 'melanoma' images compared to only 62 labeled LMM images, relying on semi-supervised pseudo-labeling to bridge this gap.
The model achieved high performance (accuracy 0.98) on a very small test set of only 64 images, which raises concerns about the robustness and statistical reliability of the results.
The study lacks external validation; the authors acknowledge the need to integrate hospital databases to determine if the model can accurately identify actual skin cancer images in clinical practice.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance comparisons were based on descriptive statistics from a single fixed test split.
The input image resolution was reduced to 32x32 pixels, which may discard fine-grained dermoscopic features critical for distinguishing between early-stage melanoma subtypes.
The source of unlabeled data included 'other websites' alongside ISIC and HAM10000, introducing potential heterogeneity and bias in the unlabeled pool used for semi-supervised learning.
